title: Marked Letters (Methylation and Gene Expression On/Off)
learning_goal: Learn that bacteria can chemically 'tag' specific short DNA motifs with a methyl group (methylation), without changing the underlying nucleotide letter, and that whether that tag gets applied can depend on the cell's environment.
simplification_note: Real bacterial methylation and gene regulation involves many interacting factors (promoter strength, transcription factors, methylation of both DNA strands, timing, etc). This level uses one well-documented type of bacterial methylation motif (recognized by Dcm-family methyltransferases, common across many bacteria and reported in some Campylobacter methylation systems) as a simplified, single-rule teaching model. The environment-dependence in Round 2 is also simplified for teaching purposes - real Campylobacter gene regulation (including phase variation and methylome changes across growth conditions) is more complex than a single on/off switch, but the core idea - that the SAME DNA sequence can be expressed differently depending on conditions - is real and important (this is 'epigenetics').
rules: This level has 2 rounds: Round 1 shows each gene's real sequence with a methylation blob marking any methylated base; Round 2 explores how environment can change the outcome.
Methylation never changes a nucleotide letter — the DNA sequence and protein stay identical either way.
A blob (🔵) means that base is really methylated. No blob means no methylation - use the motif table to understand why.
You must give an ON/OFF answer for all 3 genes in Round 1 to unlock Round 2.
In Round 2, the SAME gene can flip ON/OFF depending on the environment toggle - read the explanation before answering the final question.

motif_table.description: Material for Round 1: a table of known bacterial DNA methylation motifs, which nucleotide within the motif gets methylated, and the simplified on/off rule used in this game.

motifs.methylated_base: the first C in the motif becomes 5-methylcytosine (5mC)
motifs.game_rule: If this motif is found within the gene fragment shown, that gene's expression is switched OFF in this puzzle.
If this motif is found within the gene fragment shown, that gene's expression is switched OFF in this puzzle.

motif_table.default_rule: If NEITHER motif is present anywhere in the gene fragment, no methylation occurs and the gene's expression stays ON.

materials_provided: Each gene's real sequence, already assembled, with a methylation blob (🔵) marking any base that's actually methylated
instructions: 1. Look at the gene's sequence and the motif table. If a blob (🔵) sits on the sequence, that base is really methylated.
2. Decide: is this gene's expression ON or OFF? Use the motif table's rule to reason it out.
3. Check your answer. The game reveals the correct call and explains why.
4. Repeat for all 3 genes to unlock Round 2.
5. Toggle between 'inside the host gut' and 'outside the host' to see how the SAME gene's ON/OFF state can change depending on whether the methylation enzyme is active in that environment.
6. Answer the final question about what happens to expression when the bacterium moves environments.

jlpA.reasoning: Neither CCWGG nor GATC appears anywhere in this fragment, so no methylation switch is triggered - expression stays ON regardless of environment.

cadF.motif_found: CCAGG (a CCWGG match) at nucleotide position 19-23 (1-based)
cadF.reasoning: The CCWGG motif is present. Whether it actually gets methylated - and so whether cadF is really switched OFF - depends on whether the methyltransferase enzyme is active, which is what Round 2 explores.

ciaB.reasoning: Neither CCWGG nor GATC appears anywhere in this fragment, so no methylation switch is triggered - expression stays ON regardless of environment.

round2.title: Round 2 - Same DNA, Different Environment
round2.intro: cadF has the CCWGG motif sitting in its DNA all the time - that never changes. But having the motif is not the same as it actually being methylated. Whether the Dcm-type methyltransferase enzyme is switched on to actually place that methyl tag can depend on conditions the bacterium senses.

environments.label: Inside the host gut
environments.explanation: In this simplified scenario, conditions inside the gut activate the methyltransferase. The CCWGG motif in cadF gets methylated, and cadF expression is switched OFF.

environments.label: Outside the host (e.g. water/environment)
environments.explanation: Outside the host, the methyltransferase is inactive in this scenario. The CCWGG motif is still physically present in the DNA, but with nothing to methylate it, cadF stays unmethylated and expression is ON.

round2.unaffected_genes_note: jlpA and ciaB have no methylation motif at all in either environment, so their expression stays ON no matter what you toggle - only a gene that actually carries the motif (cadF here) can be switched by this mechanism.
round2.quiz_question: The bacterium moves from inside the host gut to outside (e.g. into water). What happens to cadF expression, and why?

quiz_options.label: Switches ON - the methyltransferase becomes inactive outside the host, so the motif no longer gets methylated
Stays OFF - once methylated, always methylated forever
Switches ON - the CCWGG motif is deleted from the DNA outside the host

round2.quiz_explanation: cadF switches back ON outside the host. The DNA sequence itself never changes - the CCWGG motif is still there. What changes is whether the methyltransferase enzyme is active to actually place the methyl tag. This is the core idea of epigenetics: the same DNA sequence can be read differently depending on chemical marks that respond to the environment.

discussion_prompt: Ask players: unlike levels 2-4, methylation doesn't change any nucleotide LETTER at all - the sequence and the protein it would make are identical in every environment. So what actually changed between Round 1 and Round 2? (Answer: nothing in the DNA sequence changed - what changed was whether the methylation MACHINERY was active. This is 'epigenetics' - control layered on top of the DNA sequence, and it can respond to environment.)
